stem: You are the GP reviewing a 26-year-old man with first-episode schizophrenia commenced on risperidone 3 mg daily 12 weeks ago. Symptoms are improved and he tolerates the medication. He has gained 6 kg since starting treatment. He smokes 10 cigarettes per day, drinks no alcohol and has no family history of cardiovascular disease. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Baseline metabolic parameters were normal. He is asymptomatic and lives independently.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/82, HR 78, RR 16, BMI 28, waist circumference 99 cm
data_table.Examination: Well-appearing, no extrapyramidal signs, no tardive dyskinesia, no orthostatic hypotension
data_table.Investigations: Baseline (12 weeks ago) fasting glucose 5.0 mmol/L, lipids normal, HbA1c 33 mmol/mol, ECG normal

lead_in: What metabolic monitoring is most appropriate at this visit?

options.text: Repeat fasting glucose and lipids at 12 months from baseline review and not before then
options.rationale: Australian guidance recommends fasting glucose and lipids at 12 weeks after starting an antipsychotic to detect early metabolic shift; 12 months is the next routine interval after that. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

options.text: Check serum prolactin and weight only because metabolic risk is minimal in young men
options.rationale: Young men are at meaningful metabolic risk on antipsychotics and a full panel is recommended at the 12-week review, not selective monitoring. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

options.text: Check fasting glucose, lipids, weight, waist and blood pressure and review the lifestyle plan
options.rationale: RANZCP and Australian guidelines recommend full metabolic monitoring at 12 weeks plus lifestyle counselling, given the early-onset weight gain seen in this patient. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

options.text: Switch to aripiprazole now to reduce future metabolic risk even though he is tolerating risperidone
options.rationale: Switching antipsychotics should be considered after weight gain is confirmed and lifestyle measures trialled; routine switch at 12 weeks is not recommended. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

options.text: Order oral glucose tolerance test plus 24-hour urinary cortisol to exclude secondary causes today
options.rationale: OGTT and cortisol are not part of routine antipsychotic monitoring; standard fasting glucose and lipid screening is the recommended baseline at 12 weeks. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Monitoring)

explanation.summary: Australian antipsychotic monitoring guidance recommends baseline assessment of weight, waist circumference, blood pressure, fasting glucose, lipids, ECG and prolactin and review at 6 weeks, 12 weeks, then yearly. Risperidone causes early weight gain and dyslipidaemia in younger patients. The 12-week review is the critical decision point: if metabolic shift is evident, intensify lifestyle measures, consider metformin, and discuss switching to a lower-risk antipsychotic such as aripiprazole if change persists. Smoking cessation should also be addressed because of independent cardiovascular and CYP1A2 effects.
key_points: Repeat metabolic panel at 6 and 12 weeks post-initiation.
Yearly metabolic review thereafter is the standard.
Intensify lifestyle measures and consider metformin early.
Switching to lower-risk agent is appropriate if changes persist.
explanation.pearls: Metformin 500 mg twice daily has good evidence for attenuating antipsychotic-induced weight gain in patients with at least 5 kg gain in 3 months; start it alongside structured lifestyle support rather than after a metabolic syndrome has developed.

subtopic_detail: Sertraline in third-trimester pregnancy and neonatal adaptation
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 31-year-old at 34 weeks pregnant with a history of moderate depression. She has been on sertraline 100 mg daily for 18 months and is well, with no current symptoms. She is anxious about effects on the baby and considering stopping the medication before delivery. She has no other medications, no risk of postpartum psychosis and has supportive family. She has booked at a tertiary centre.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 78, T 36.6 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Soft uterus, cephalic, foetal heart 138 reassuring, no oedema, EPDS score 7

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding sertraline continuation in late pregnancy?

options.text: Stop sertraline now to reduce neonatal adaptation syndrome risk and restart after delivery completed
options.rationale: Stopping antidepressants in late pregnancy carries a substantial relapse risk in postpartum depression and is not recommended in stable, well patients. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal)

options.text: Continue sertraline 100 mg daily and arrange neonatal adaptation review at 24 to 48 hours after birth
options.rationale: Australian practice is to continue effective antidepressants through pregnancy and breastfeeding, with paediatric review for neonatal adaptation syndrome in the first 24 to 48 hours after birth. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal)

options.text: Halve the sertraline dose now and stop it completely two weeks before the expected date of delivery
options.rationale: Tapering before delivery to reduce neonatal symptoms increases maternal relapse risk and is not recommended in well stable patients on a maintenance SSRI. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal)

options.text: Switch sertraline to fluoxetine to maintain a longer half-life and reduce neonatal withdrawal effects
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has a long half-life and may extend exposure rather than reduce neonatal symptoms; switching well-tolerated treatment in late pregnancy is not recommended. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal)

options.text: Substitute psychological therapy alone now and discontinue sertraline before the third trimester begins
options.rationale: Switching from effective pharmacotherapy to psychological therapy alone late in pregnancy risks relapse; both modalities can be combined when clinically indicated. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal)

explanation.summary: Australian perinatal psychiatry guidance favours continuing effective antidepressants in pregnancy because relapse risk often outweighs the small absolute risk of neonatal adaptation syndrome. Sertraline is one of the SSRIs with the most reassuring breastfeeding data. Neonates exposed in the third trimester should be observed for 24 to 48 hours for jitteriness, feeding difficulties and respiratory symptoms. Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn is a rare and largely transient association. A maternal mental health plan and EPDS monitoring postpartum are routine.
key_points: Continue effective antidepressants in stable pregnant women.
Watch for neonatal adaptation in the first 24 to 48 hours.
Sertraline and paroxetine have lower breast milk concentrations.
Add EPDS at 6 weeks and 3 months postpartum to detect relapse.
explanation.pearls: Neonatal adaptation syndrome occurs in around 30 percent of SSRI-exposed infants, peaks at 24 to 48 hours and resolves within a week without specific treatment; rooming-in plus paediatric observation is sufficient in most term babies.

subtopic_detail: Valproate pregnancy prevention in bipolar disorder
stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 23-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder who has been stable for 18 months on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily. She has a long-term partner and uses condoms only. She is not currently planning pregnancy but is unsure about her contraception. She is aware valproate has risks in pregnancy. She has no other psychiatric or medical conditions and her last manic episode required hospital admission.

data_table.Examination: Stable mood, euthymic, no extrapyramidal signs, no tremor
data_table.Investigations: FBC, LFTs, valproate level within range; beta-hCG negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding her treatment plan?

options.text: Reassure that condoms are adequate contraception and continue valproate at the current dose long-term
options.rationale: Valproate has a 10 percent major congenital malformation and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental impairment risk; highly effective contraception is required throughout treatment. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Plan a slow valproate taper over four weeks and start lamotrigine 25 mg daily titrating up cautiously
options.rationale: Tapering a stabilising mood stabiliser without secured alternative cover risks severe relapse with prior psychiatric hospitalisation; secure contraception is the immediate priority. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Switch to lithium today and provide both verbal and written information about teratogenicity risks
options.rationale: Switching mood stabilisers in a stable patient introduces relapse risk; the immediate need is highly effective contraception while continuing the stabilising agent. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Continue valproate and add a combined oral contraceptive without other contraceptive review or plan
options.rationale: Combined oral contraception alone meets effectiveness criteria but the pregnancy prevention programme requires annual review and documented risk acknowledgement, not contraception alone. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Insert an etonogestrel subdermal implant today and document the pregnancy prevention programme plan
options.rationale: Australian and TGA-aligned pregnancy prevention programme requires highly effective contraception such as a LARC plus documented annual risk acknowledgement when valproate is used in women of reproductive potential. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate carries a 10 percent risk of major congenital malformation and 30 to 40 percent risk of neurodevelopmental impairment with foetal exposure. Australian pregnancy prevention programme guidance requires highly effective contraception such as long-acting reversible contraception, annual reauthorisation, and documented patient acknowledgement of risks. In women planning pregnancy, switch to a safer mood stabiliser such as lithium or lamotrigine before conception. Folic acid 5 mg daily is added if conception is planned despite valproate.
key_points: Use LARC for highly effective contraception on valproate.
Document the pregnancy prevention plan and review annually.
Switch to safer agents before planned conception.
Prescribe 5 mg folic acid daily if conception is planned.
explanation.pearls: The risk of neural tube defects with valproate exposure is highest in the first 28 days; brief pre-conception switching may not eliminate risk if the embryo is already implanted, so LARC is preferable to depending on a planned switch alone.

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman on the medical ward. She has had no significant medical history but has become mute, immobile and resistant to passive limb movement over the last 24 hours. Her examination shows waxy flexibility, mutism and posturing without focal neurological signs. She has had two previous episodes of psychotic depression. CT head and basic metabolic screen are unremarkable. NMS, encephalitis and serotonin syndrome are not supported by current findings.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 88, RR 16, BP 124/74, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mutism, immobility, waxy flexibility, posturing of left arm; no rigidity, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: CT head normal; FBC, U&E, LFT, glucose, calcium, magnesium normal; CK 220, urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic and therapeutic step?

options.text: Order a lumbar puncture and MRI brain before commencing any specific pharmacological therapy today
options.rationale: Imaging and CSF analysis are appropriate when organic causes are suspected, but a lorazepam challenge can proceed in parallel because basic screening is normal. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

options.text: Begin oral olanzapine 10 mg now to treat presumed underlying psychosis prior to other interventions
options.rationale: Antipsychotics in untreated catatonia can precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome; benzodiazepines or ECT are first-line until catatonia resolves. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

options.text: Refer for urgent electroconvulsive therapy without trial of any benzodiazepine challenge first
options.rationale: ECT is reserved for malignant or benzodiazepine-resistant catatonia; a lorazepam challenge should be tried first because most catatonia responds to benzodiazepines. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

options.text: Commence intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg as needed for behavioural control on the ward overnight
options.rationale: High-potency antipsychotics in catatonia significantly increase the risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome and worsen the catatonia itself. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

options.text: Administer intravenous lorazepam 2 mg and reassess catatonia features after 10 minutes
options.rationale: An IV lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic; reduction in Bush Francis catatonia features within 10 minutes supports the diagnosis and guides ongoing benzodiazepine use. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a clinical syndrome of stupor, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility and other motor features that can occur with mood, psychotic and organic disorders. Intravenous or intramuscular lorazepam at 1 to 2 mg gives a 60 to 80 percent response within 10 minutes and is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Antipsychotics should be withheld until catatonia resolves because they predispose to neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Failure to respond to benzodiazepine should prompt early ECT under the relevant mental health act framework.
key_points: Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is the recommended challenge dose.
Antipsychotics worsen catatonia and increase NMS risk.
Reserve ECT for benzodiazepine-resistant or malignant catatonia.
Investigate for organic and mood disorder causes simultaneously.
explanation.pearls: Persistent autonomic instability, hyperthermia above 38 °C or rising CK in a catatonic patient signals malignant catatonia; transfer to ICU and arrange ECT urgently because untreated mortality exceeds 50 percent.

subtopic_detail: ECT capacity and consent in severe depression
stem: You are the inpatient psychiatry registrar in Victoria seeing a 68-year-old woman with severe depression with psychotic features admitted under the Mental Health and Wellbeing Act. She has nihilistic delusions, food refusal, BMI 16 and a clear treatment plan. She believes she does not deserve treatment and refuses ECT. Her family supports treatment. She has been trialled on adequate doses of two antidepressants without benefit. She has previously responded well to ECT and is otherwise medically fit.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/68, HR 64, BMI 16, T 36.4 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Psychomotor retardation, mute on some questions, oriented when responsive, dehydrated mucosae
data_table.Investigations: FBC, U&E, LFT normal; ECG normal sinus rhythm; CT head no acute change

lead_in: What is the most appropriate process to proceed with ECT?

options.text: Proceed with ECT under family verbal consent because she lacks insight into her own illness now
options.rationale: Substitute consent from family is not sufficient; mental health legislation requires formal capacity assessment and tribunal review before involuntary ECT in adults. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria

options.text: Defer ECT and continue antidepressant titration despite worsening starvation and clear nihilistic refusal
options.rationale: Continued pharmacotherapy in severe psychotic depression with starvation and previous ECT response delays effective treatment and risks death by inanition. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria

options.text: Assess capacity, document refusal, and apply to the Mental Health Tribunal for ECT authorisation
options.rationale: Australian mental health acts require a formal capacity assessment and Tribunal hearing for involuntary ECT in adults; this preserves rights while allowing life-saving treatment. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria

options.text: Refer to the public guardian for substitute decision making about ongoing antipsychotic treatment options
options.rationale: Public Guardian decision making applies to general medical treatment in adults without capacity but ECT specifically requires Tribunal authorisation under mental health acts. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria

options.text: Administer ECT under the emergency duty of care provisions without any further legal or consent process
options.rationale: Emergency duty of care covers immediate medical emergencies; planned ECT requires formal legal process and Tribunal authorisation when the patient refuses. (Source: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria)
source_refs: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria

explanation.summary: Australian mental health legislation in all states requires formal assessment of capacity and tribunal authorisation for ECT in adult patients who refuse or cannot consent. Capacity has four elements: understanding, retaining, using and weighing, and communicating the decision. Severe psychotic depression with nihilistic delusions often impairs capacity for treatment decisions. A documented assessment, second psychiatric opinion, and tribunal hearing usually within several working days authorise treatment while protecting patient rights. Family input is sought but is not legally sufficient on its own.
key_points: Capacity has four legal elements to assess and document.
Tribunal authorisation is required for involuntary ECT.
A second psychiatric opinion supports the application.
Family consent is not legally sufficient for adult ECT.
explanation.pearls: Severe melancholic depression with food refusal has a 30-day mortality rate of around 15 percent untreated; document this clearly in the Tribunal application because the immediate physical risk often justifies a same-week hearing.

sources.label: Mental Health and Wellbeing Act Victoria
